reasoning: Applied error taxonomy to 18 identified errors, selecting 7 questions targeting the 4 highest-priority error patterns: past tense narration (exercise_priority 4-5, 6 instances), 3rd person -s agreement (priority 4, 4 instances), article omission (priority 3-4, 3 instances), and plural -s omission (priority 3-4, 4 instances). ZPD calibration: A1 lower bound to A1+/pre-A2 upper bound. Distribution: 2 zpd_lower (recognition/confidence), 3 zpd_target (boundary practice), 2 zpd_stretch (production). Question types mapped per error-to-question table: verb tense -> gap(blank), SVA -> choice/gap(choices), articles -> gap(choices), plurals -> sorter/categorizer. Distractors drawn from student's actual errors and Ukrainian L1 transfer patterns (present-for-past, zero-article, have-for-age, on-for-to). Lesson context (shopping, food, family) used throughout for relevance.

payload.title: How do we say someone's age in English?
payload.explanation: In English we say 'She IS seven years old', not 'She HAS seven years'. Ukrainian uses 'maty roky' (to have years), but English uses 'to be' + age.
payload.content: In the lesson you said: 'She have seven years'

payload.title: Choose the correct preposition
payload.template_text: On Saturday I went {0} the shop.
payload.explanation: We say 'go TO the shop', not 'go ON shop'. In Ukrainian 'na' can mean both 'on' and 'to', but in English they are different words.
payload.content: In the lesson you said: 'I go on shop'

payload.title: Past tense: What did you do at the weekend?
payload.template_text: Last weekend I {0} to the park with my children.
payload.explanation: 'Went' is the past tense of 'go'. When talking about last weekend, use past tense: I went, I bought, I cooked.
payload.content: In the lesson you said: 'I go to park'

source_ref.corrected: My family likes it very much

payload.title: Third person -s: Complete the sentences
payload.template_text: My family {0} borscht very much. My daughter {1} cheese. The bread {2} one pound.
payload.explanation: For he/she/it and singular nouns, add -s to the verb: likes, costs, wants. 'My family' and 'my daughter' and 'the bread' are all singular.
payload.content: In the lesson you said: 'My family like', 'bread cost', 'the cheese cost'

payload.title: Articles: a, an, or some?
payload.template_text: This is {0} apple. I want {1} milk. Can I have {2} tomato?
payload.explanation: Use 'an' before vowel sounds (an apple). Use 'a' before consonant sounds (a tomato). Use 'some' for uncountable nouns (some milk). Ukrainian has no articles, so this needs practice!
payload.content: In the lesson you said: 'This is apple' (missing 'an')

payload.title: Sort: Does the noun need plural -s after a number?
payload.explanation: Countable nouns add -s after numbers: two pounds, three tomatoes, four apples, six eggs. Uncountable nouns use 'some' and never add -s: some milk, some bread.

source_ref.original: children play. I sit and look
source_ref.corrected: The children played. I sat and watched

payload.title: Past tense: Tell the story of your weekend
payload.template_text: Last Saturday I {0} chicken for dinner. Then on Sunday I {1} to the park. The children {2} and I {3} and watched them.
payload.explanation: When telling a story about the past, use past tense for every verb: cooked (cook+ed), went (irregular), played (play+ed), sat (irregular: sit->sat).
payload.content: In the lesson you said: 'children play, I sit and look' - these should be past tense
